paragraphs.body: Excerpt #1

(Note: This is the Opening/Prologue/First Chapter of my Novel. Lots of meeting characters/setting up themes & settings & tone & voice etc., Excited to get opinions on all of this  and any other thoughts <3)

… to pinpoint exactly where in one’s life everything changed, where somewhere something cracked and therein continued to break and crack and eventually crumble at the edges until it is a race to catch the original foliage in hopes of putting it back in place. Who's responsible for working the wall back together? Who is meant to stand guard and sense when the earth moves? Who is meant to have eyes so strong as to see the tiniest crack?

annotations.comment: I feel and appreciate your desire for a poetic opening, but this mostly didn’t land for me. There’s a lot of metaphor and some of it doesn’t quite connect (foliage doesn’t make a wall, for example). I also don’t really think childhood is simple (maybe this narrator does, incorrectly, but since I’m not sure who’s speaking here, can’t tell). All of that said, I love the line “Dottie Clarke doesn’t…” and think it would be a terrific opening.

paragraphs.body: Childhood can be so simple, as such. Eyes still so soft with curiosity and ignorance. Most children can have the luxury of overlooking. The adults in their lives, their guardians, can protect them until it is time for them to grow up.

Dottie Clarke doesn’t remember exactly when she grew up. She was always her father’s special little girl and his family was always the center of his world. He spends every waking moment working only for her, for his entire perfect little family he cherishes every exacerbated breath. Every morning he awakens, and every long day he hauls through until his body finally gives out to sleep before his restless mind can stop him again. He does it for his family, his precious, ignorant children, whose eyes light up and voices float into the air whenever he comes from work. The pain of the day melts away, even the tiresome ache in his bones when his youngest son runs into his arm. But Dottie sees her father's eyes just before he spots little Benjamin Jr. She notices how hard it is for him to kneel. She didn’t notice when she was little when she was Thomas Jr’s age, but she sees it now. That’s why she doesn’t run into her Daddy’s arms that often anymore. She’s too big now.

annotations.comment: The jump to present tense here was confusing. Is he in the past or now?

Not quite the right word. You notice that you’ve asserted that the father does everything twice in two sentences. Is the father going to be important in the story? If not, we may not need to spend so much time with him.
Okay, this seems like it’s in Dottie’s childhood (not grown up yet) so a little confused on the timing, and it seems to work against your assertion that she was slightly sheltered and spoiled?
Okay, I think I’m caught up with the timeline. But how old is she?

paragraphs.body: A typical morning, Dottie’s cracked open eyes along with the dawn as it peeks through her dusty window. On a typical Sunday morning, she knows everyone else will want to sleep in. But the sun is shining more and more rays across her little room, she watches as it creeps across her floor, alights the walls, onto her thin paisley quilt, until it meets her hands, over her face… She’s fully awake, the day is here, and there’s no sense in trying to go back to sleep now.

annotations.comment: Notice how you’re kind of working out what day of the week it is here.

paragraphs.body: The bed is hot. Dottie can feel her nightgown sticking to her skin, but it’s also so comfy. She feels like one of farmer Clarence’s pigs sleeping in a warm mud bath, her bones stiff and sluggish, with another pig lying on top of her back. Recently, at bedtime, Benjamin Jr. has been insisting that he does not need his big sister’s cuddles to help him fall asleep anymore. In the summertime, Dottie doesn’t mind, their bedroom in the attic tends to get pretty warm once the sun comes up. But somehow, without fail, Dottie always wakes up with Thomas Jr. on her side of the bed.

annotations.comment: Townsperson
This is a great description. Again, the story comes alive for me when you share specific details and put me in a scene.
I kinda didn’t get that he was in the bed until here.

paragraphs.body: Slowly and gently, Dottie manages to stuff her pillow in between her little brother's arms. She held her breath as a tiny groan escaped, but Benjamin Jr. soon snuggled his nose and cheeks into the thin pillow stuffing, inhaling his sister’s scent, and relaxed. No longer a toddler, her brother lost most of his adorable baby fat in his arm and belly, but Dottie could still somewhat see his cute baby face in the apple of his cheeks.

annotations.comment: Switching around in verb tense.
Red Hair/Curls?

paragraphs.body: When Mother Mae suggested that Benjamin Jr could sleep upstairs with Dottie, she was overjoyed at the idea. The attic bedroom gets chilly in the wintertime, and there are still nights when the rough rustling of the big red oak against her window outside makes Dottie’s imagination run wild. And though Thomas Jr. was no longer “a baby,” he will always be Dottie’s sweet baby brother that she loves to coddle. At least, until he becomes too big to mind her…

Ready to escape the heat of her room, Dottie gently tiptoes her barefoot feet across the creaky floor, slowly opens, steps through, and closes the small door, then quickly walks down the steps. The heat of the house below is a touch cooler as the space is a bit larger, but it still feels sticky and thick. Hoping to go outside to get some cool well water and fresh air, Dottie starts to tiptoe for the front door.
But then she hears little Maggie’s whimper.

Dottie takes a deep breath, chooses to ignore it, and moves on. But she forgets her morning manners, and her quickened pace steps onto a particularly angry piece of floorboard and it lets out a creek! Dottie winces and freezes - the sound feels like it echoed throughout the entire house. It doesn’t take much, the walls are thin and aside from the attic bedroom, everyone else sleeps a hair's-width from the kitchen and the front door.

annotations.comment: Love this way of referring to it. Seems ominous.

paragraphs.body: Dottie holds her breath and waits for somebody to wake up and complain.
The only one complaining is little Maggie, whose whimper has turned into a full-blown whine. She obviously knows that Dottie is awake, and she obviously wants somebody to help her out of her crib.

Why isn’t anyone else waking up? Dottie relaxes for a moment… only to feel trapped, so she listens to little Maggie cry. Then start to sob. And when the toddler starts using her little lungs to scream… “...Dot-Dot…” Dottie finally huffs and turns to her big brother's room.

annotations.comment: I was so confused as to why this child is in the brother’s room.

paragraphs.body: Morning manners forgotten, Dottie opens Ben and Mary’s door, tugging roughly to rip the sticky door from its frame, letting it swing open and bang against opposite wall. But inside the tiny, sun-filled room, the only one complaining is little Maggie. Dottie doesn’t have to give her brother and sister-in-law more than a quick glance to see that they are still fast asleep, despite their daughter’s cries at the foot of their bed. Maggie is desperate to get out of her crib. It seems only yesterday that she started standing when truly that was several months ago. Now, all she wants to do is stand and follow Dottie around the house, until she tires little legs out. And this is how Dottie usually finds her in the morning; red-faced, tears streaming down her cheeks, legs bouncing, one hand rapping against the crib bar and the other reaching out to be touched. None of it woke up her parents.

annotations.comment: Ah, okay.

paragraphs.body: Once Maggie’s sleepy, tear-wet eyes spot Dottie, “Dot-Dot! Dot-Dot! Dot-Dot!”

Dottie puts a finger to her lips to gently shush her niece, but a small smile escapes. She can’t be mad at such a sweet face, especially when she’s so desperate to be picked up. Dottie looks at her unmoving brother and sister-in-law. So Dottie slides around the bed, wincing a touch when Mary rolls over and meets Maggie at the edge of her crib.

annotations.text: and meets Maggie at the edge of her crib
annotations.comment: Not quite following what’s going on here.

paragraphs.body: “Good morning, Maggie!” Dottie excitedly whispers as she gently kisses the toddler's wispy blonde hair. Dottie immediately leans edge of the crib to securely wrap her arms around Maggie’s little torso. Maggie in response digs her wet hands into Dottie’s nightgown, but her tears seem to calm a bit. Dottie remembers the first time Benjamin Jr. climbed out of the same crib, luckily Dottie was home and stopped before he even got his other foot over the rail. But it happened two more times, and the third time, Mother Mae was resting and Thomas Jr. nearly cracked his head open. That was when Papa agreed to let Thomas Jr. sleep with Dottie.
And by that point, Mary was pregnant. An empty crib was needed.

But Benjamin was two and a half when he started climbing out. Maggie just turned two. And Dottie is not taking any chances. She used to fantasize about having sleepovers in the attic with Thomas Jr. and Maggie. But this past summer with Thomas Jr. has proven to be a bit uncomfortable. And three kids in one bed instead of two…

annotations.comment: How old is Dottie?

paragraphs.body: “Did you sleep well?” whispers Dottie, rubbing Maggie back.
“Mm… hmm…” Maggie manages a response amongst her receding tears.
“What’s wrong? Mm? Did you have a bad dream?” With her bottom lip pouting, Maggie shakes her head. “Well then what’s wrong, hm?”
The little girl just plays with the neckline of her young aunt’s nightgown.
“.... Dot-Dot…” The toddler whine-whispers, traces of still sleep in her voice.
“Yes?”
Maggie’s wet eyes make her loose little head look around the room. Unsatisfied, she opens her mouth to show her two front teeth and lets out another whine.

“Hey, hey, hey, now! Shh… I know you want Mommy and Daddy but… but they’re really sleepy right now,” Dottie runs her knuckles over Maggie’s perfect, soft cheeks, hoping to soothe her. “Do you think you can go back to sleep? It’s too early for little Maggies.”

annotations.comment: I wondered why Dottie doesn’t want to wake the parents up. This seems unusually altruistic of her for a (?) teenager.

paragraphs.body: Again, Maggie shakes her head.

Dottie sighs and looks back at the sleeping couple behind her, debating what to do. Sunday is one of the only mornings her big brother can truly sleep in, until the entire family is whisked away to church that is. But bless him, he’s almost falling off the bed with his long limbs. It always takes him so long to fall asleep, to get comfortable. Even though he comes home tired, wakes up looking forward to going back to bed, his young body is just so sore. Most nights, the dinner plate Dottie makes goes cold before he makes it home. By then, he’s not interested in eating, or bathing, or holding his daughter, she just wants to try and rest for the next day out in the fields. And then there’s Mary, squeezing her pillow so close to her chest and curled up on her side of bed like she’s guarding something.

annotations.comment: You might play with intertwining your dialogue and information a bit more. Right now, we read the dialogue and have questions, then read the explanation that answers the question. It might work better to have the answer come in the dialogue. (I can explain what I mean!)
annotations.context_left: Dottie sighs and looks back at the sleeping couple behind her, debating what to do. Sunday
annotations.context_right: one of the only mornings her big brother can truly sleep in, until the entire family is whisked awa

annotations.comment: Something to note is that I’m not sure where you conflict is so far. Dottie seems to have some issues with the dad, but he’s not really in the story. Everyone else, she seems to be fine with, and she doesn’t seem especially resentful of her role in life or anything like that. So, what’s the plot?

paragraphs.body: “Sunshine…” Dottie turns back to Maggie has her little finger outstretched to the sunbeams coming through the window.

“You like that? You want to like the pretty sunshine, hm?” Maggie nods but sticks her thumb as more tears start to leak from her eyes, “Silly girl,” Dottie starts to tickle under her arms, “Aren’t you a silly girl?” until a joyous, shrill laugh escapes from Maggie.
“Ah, gee Dot!” The sluggish voice of her brother arises from the bed, “If you’re gonna make her louder, just take her in the kitchen, will ya? We’re tryin’ to sleep!”
“If you wanted her to go back to sleep,” Dottie picks her niece and rests her on her hip, keeping her back to her brother, “why didn’t one of you comfort her?”
“Come on, Dot… ya know what Mother Mae said, ‘If you keep babyin’ ‘er, the longer she’ll be a baby!’”

annotations.comment: I would suggest not rendering the brother’s dialogue in a dialect. It’s harder to read, and seems odd that only he has it.

paragraphs.body: “That’s not what she said,” Dot disagreed in a sing-songy voice as she bounced Maggie.
“Not exactly…” Dot hears her brother roll over in bed, “but she said something,” he yawns, “like that…”
“She also hates having to listen to a baby cry, especially when she’s trying to sleep, Benji-”

“Oh quit whining-”
	“Can you both of you take this outside,” Mary’s gentle voice wakes up, startling Dottie and Ben, “You’re giving me a headache with all this nagging.”
Ben and Dottie watch her snuggle back into her pillow before offering a, “Sorry, Mary.” Dottie watches Ben comfortingly rub his wife on the back.

annotations.comment: I’m really wondering why the mother of this child doesn’t have to look after her and why the other two apologize to her for waking her up?

paragraphs.body: “Dot,” Ben starts, voice more gentle, “can you please just…” he rubs just his eyes in frustration, “I’ll come and take her in a minute, I just need-”
“No, it’s fine, Benji, I just-” Dottie’s eyes wander to Mary before correcting herself back to Ben’s, “You need your sleep, and it’s Sunday. You have time before breakfast.”
“You sure?” Her brother looks her straight in the eye.
Dottie easily smiles, “Of course. I was going to go outside anyway. And I think Maggie wants to go outside, too. Huh, Mags? Want to go outside?”
“Out, out!” Maggie bounces her little legs and points her toes in excitement.
Dottie adjusts the wiggly toddler in arms before confidently looking at her brother, “I’m already up and I like to spend time with her. We’ll see you at breakfast.”

annotations.comment: Who’s going to cook this breakfast? What time do they have to be at church?

paragraphs.body: As Dottie starts to leave with Maggie, she feels her brother’s solemn eyes on his little daughter. Dottie lifts one of Maggie’s little arms, “Say ‘Go sleepy-time, Daddy! I’ll see you at breakfast’”
A little giggle from Maggie allows Ben to relax. He moves to wrap his arms around his wife and pull her close, “We’ll be out in a bit, Dot. Don’t have too much fun without us.”
But Mary nudges him with her elbow, “Move! It’s too hot to be this close!” Ben lets out a huff in the argument but she continues, “Dottie, I’ll be out to help you with breakfast I promise…”

annotations.comment: Boy, I do not like Mary.

paragraphs.body: Dottie watches as Mary easily falls back into sleep, Ben rolls over to begrudgingly finds a new comfortable position.

Outside, the sunshine is twice as bright as it has fully risen over the fields. But the air is sweet and open, still warm under a new, June sun but not suffocating. Not like inside the house. Outside, Dottie can breathe. If only she didn’t hate the bugs summer brings.

annotations.comment: hint mountain / location

paragraphs.body: She smacks at a flying critter coming too close to her face.
“Lay-bugs!” Maggie starts kicking her legs again.
“Oh, I don’t think those are ladybugs. And you don’t have shoes on. Come on, Mother Mae will be happy for some fresh, cool water before breakfast.”
Dottie didn’t even get the first bucket of water inside before she heard Mother Mae’s voice, “... Dorothy! Dorothy Florence Clarke! Dorothy, where are you?”
“I’m outside, Mother Mae!” Dottie attempts to shout, but the strain of balancing a heavy bucket of water, Maggie on her hip and running makes it difficult to breathe.

Once Dottie makes it inside the house, she sees her stepmother hobbling down the attic stairs whilst dangling Benjamin Jr. by the arm like a prized turkey. “Oh, Dorothy, where did you go, it’s not even seven o’clock yet! You nearly gave me a heart attack!”
“Sorry, Mother Mae. I was really hot and couldn’ really get back to sleep, so I wanted to grab some well water before breakfast.”

Mother Mae takes a deep breath, “Could not, sweetheart. You could not really get back to sleep. Don’t let this place make you lose your proper English like it did for your brother. I can only hope that we can teach Benjamin Jr. and Maggie to speak correctly even if they were born here.”

annotations.comment: This correction seems strange to me.

paragraphs.body: “Yes, ma’am.” Dottie can’t help but smile at her flustered stepmother. She looks like an angry chicken sometimes after it’s been chased by an excited fox. She just wants to strut and fluff out her feathers in everyone's face.

“Yes, well, this one,” Mother Mae pinches the cheek of Benjamin Jr., making her son squeal, run around Dottie, and hide under the kitchen table with a giggle, “was jumping on my bed this morning and was singing ‘Oh! Susanna’ at the top of his lungs until my eyes opened and I got out of bed,” Mother Mae places a hand on her popped out hip to add to her disapproving face, “I assume he was looking for his father but for some reason, Dorothy,” now she crosses her arms and demands Dottie’s eye contact, “your father wasn’t in bed last night.”
The woman looked down at her young stepdaughter, expecting an answer.
Dottie’s mouth is dry with a loss for knowledge, “I don’t… know?”

annotations.comment: Okay, here we have the hint of something being really wrong.

paragraphs.body: “Hmm… you don’t know or you won’t tell me?” Mother Mae leans an arm against the kitchen table as if to test Dottie, I dare you to try and run from this.
Of course, Dottie has an idea of where her father is. But then again, so does Mother Mae, otherwise neither of them would be standing there. The females stare at each other and breathe.

annotations.comment: Great line.

paragraphs.body: “Fine, if you don’t know then…” Mother Mae steps around Dottie, allowing Dottie to yet again relax so she sets Maggie in her high chair. However, “I suppose we should start breakfast, not sure how in the world we’re supposed to get ready for church when half of the people in this house cannot get out of bed to help us make breakfast let alone get ready for church,” Dottie starts to grab the mixing bowls and pans from the cupboard, all while nodding, “I mean, really Dorothy, by the time we are done cooking, will they even be awake, only God knows! Honestly,” she grabs her apron off its hook on the wall and ties it around her waist, “Mary should be the one up and about, making breakfast this morning, not you, with how much babysitting you do with little Maggie. I told your father, those two should have gotten their own home when they got married, but no! No, they’re too young, he says. Well, maybe he’s right, but now Benjamin's stepmother and little sister’s caring for his daughter more than wife is, now what’s the logic in that?

annotations.comment: Yeah, my thought too.

paragraphs.body: Dottie just shrugs her shoulders, distracting herself by trying to start a fire in their little hearth.

annotations.comment: To my mind, I kind of agree with the stepmother. But it seems like Dottie maybe does not?

paragraphs.body: “And by the time they do wake up and finish eating we won’t have time to get ready for church which means I won’t have time to get ready for church and I looked so dreadful last week, you when you get to be my age you need to actually start caring about what you look like, so a lovely man, like you father, with want to marry you. But even after that happens…”

annotations.comment: I do feel like the stepmother is faking into a bit of a trope — the man-crazy, slightly bitchy, nagging woman no one likes.

paragraphs.body: Dottie never understands why Mother Mae cares so much about her looks, she always looks beautiful, that’s why Papa married her. Of course, she looks nothing like Dottie’s mother. Mother Mae has these large, dark eyes with perfect long eyelashes. And her hair is big and poofy with perfect ringlets. Mother Mae always claims it’s her biggest setback, her curls, but Dottie wishes she had gorgeous hair like that. She wonders if that is what made her father fall in love with her. Because she looks so different from her mother…

annotations.comment: Might be a good time to let us know what happened to the mom.

paragraphs.body: “Dorothy, dearest, I hope you know what you are wearing today… What are you doing?”
Dottie didn’t even realize she had started making her father’s morning cup of coffee.
“Who is that for?”
“Um… Papa?”
“I thought you didn’t know where he was…”

annotations.comment: Confused by this.

paragraphs.body: Again, the silent staring.
“Very well, Dorothy. But if you’re going to make him some, and make it a small cup, we're getting low, make me one as well. I will need it in order to face Minnie and Ida today.”
Dorothy smiles. She spots her brother under the kitchen table again and she gets an idea, “Benjamin Jr. and I can start breakfast if you want to start getting ready.”
“No dear, I couldn’t ask that of you,” but Dottie knows she’s won from the bright look in her stepmother’s eyes.
“No, it’s alright, you’ll feel better once I get the coffee ready and you get dressed. Everyone should be up by then, right?”
Mother Mae smiles, grabs Dottie’s face and kisses her head, “This is why you are my favorite.”

annotations.comment: Olivia, a lot of interesting writing here. I can see you teaching yourself about these characters, and learning about their lives and setting, and so on. I think there’s a lot of potential here. So far, I don’t quite see who these people are, or what the plot of the story will be. I think this is partly because of the narrative stance you’ve chosen, in which the narrator doesn’t have much insight into the feelings of the people in the story. So I wonder if you might play with what Dottie is thinking and feeling, not just doing — when she gets caught making the cup of coffee, for example, is she like, OH SHIT, or is she like, Whatever, stepmom. We need some insight, especially since the conflict seems to be mostly with the dad, who isn’t in this excerpt much. I’d also encourage you to think a bit more outside the box with your characters. Everyone seems to be in a familiar character groove — hardworking young husband, slightly vapid stepmom, lazy new mom, and I’m having trouble figuring them out beyond that. Let’s take Mary, for example. Is she depressed? If so, it would make sense why Dottie is so nice to her. Or is she entitled and lazy? If so, why doesn’t Dottie quite see that? You made a point at the beginning of the excerpt that Dottie has grown up, but I don’t see that world-weariness in her actions yet. She seems young and dutiful. All of that said, please keep going — I’m really interested in this family and can’t wait to see what you find for them to do!
